feed.title: Stories by Samantha Chu on Medium
feed.description: Stories by Samantha Chu on Medium

items.title: User and Algorithm Analysis: Instagram Advertisements
items.author: Samantha Chu
items.description:
Looking into how Instagram filters its advertisement content for its variety of users and analyzing the ways users interact and respond to them.

Anna Shats, Sophia Wang, Chloe Wang, and Samantha Chu are first-year M.P.S. students studying Information Science at Cornell University.

Project Scope

Over the last five years, the popularity of Instagram as a content-creating platform has permeated the online social media market, and become one of the biggest ways in which users can create a digital presence through personal expression on the internet. In the world today, the use of Instagram as a marketing and product-promoting tool is conductive to product success, especially in businesses that target ages 34 and younger, as this age group makes up over half of Instagram’s user base.

The Intelligent User Interface we have chosen to analyze is advertisements on Instagram. We want to look into how advertisements are curated toward each individual profile, and what kind of data is collected in order to decide what ads are shown. In addition, we are interested in observing how users of Instagram interact with the advertisements and how they feel about the ad content that is shown to them.

Our team was interested in researching the different components for what goes into displaying advertisements to users on Instagram. Since the four of us are users of the Instagram platform ourselves, we have experience with interacting with the newsfeed platform and have also been exposed to Instagram’s ad algorithm. We are interested in the research questions that explore how Instagram curates its advertisements for each individual user and maximizes its profit from sponsors. After doing initial research, we found that within the last few years, the number of ads that Instagram shows its users has increased. Specifically, in late summer of 2016, Instagram altered its platform to allow any user to advertise on Instagram, rather than just established brands with large followings. We also considered other popular forms of social media platforms to research, such as Snapchat, TikTok, and Facebook, but ultimately decided to zero in on the Instagram platform because we found its methods of displaying advertisements to be the most ubiquitous throughout the platform, as we noticed ads displayed on both stories and the newsfeed.

With these interests in mind, we developed three questions to focus on and to address in our case study:

  1. Are different advertisement categories (i.e. Sports, Fashion, Food, etc.) prioritized amongst different Instagram users? If so, what behavior and contextual clues does Instagram employ to identify the values in their users?
  2. What types of data does Instagram/Facebook collect from its users to curate the advertisements for individuals?
  3. How do Instagram users respond to the different advertisements that appear on their Instagram newsfeed and on their Instagram stories? Do users tend to engage or respond more favorably to certain types of ad delivery (i.e. videos, photos, surveys) over others?

Instagram employs two different channels to display ads: stories and posts. Oftentimes ads are paid for by other users on Instagram who want to promote their brand. These users need to designate their profile as a “business profile” in order to have their ads shown to other users. Instagram also provides the option to add links to stories and posts that direct the user to the website to purchase the item. This feature allows users to easily promote their website through the app. Advertisers have the option of curating their ad content and choosing what they want to market to their customers. Our team is interested in researching the user-centered aspect of advertisements, and conducting user research interviews to gain a wider perspective about how users react to the advertisements that are shown to them. In addition, we want to research the algorithm-side of Instagram ads, and learn more about how Instagram filters its ad content to its users and how the system chooses what ads to show and which ads to hide.

Analytic/Evaluation Methods

Evaluation Questions: User Interview Guide

Review of Literature

For answering the first two questions “Are different advertisement categories (i.e. Sports, Fashion, Food, etc.) prioritized amongst different Instagram users? If so, what behavior and contextual clues does Instagram employ to identify the values in their users?” and “What types of data does Instagram/Facebook collect from its users to curate the advertisements for individuals?”, we decided to conduct a literature review of articles and papers analyzing advertising algorithms on social media platforms. This is a reasonable evaluation technique for answering this question since we do not have access to the actual algorithm instagram uses, we still want to be able to see how transparent the platform is regarding the components that they use for deciding how to cater their advertisements.

User Interviews

We decided to conduct user interviews for answering the question of “How do Instagram users respond to the different advertisements that appear on their Instagram newsfeed and on their Instagram stories? Do users tend to engage or respond more favorably to certain types of ad delivery (i.e. videos, photos, surveys) over others? ”. The reason we chose to conduct user interviews is to get a general overview of users reactions to ads on the Instagram platform as well as gage their reactions to the two venues through which they receive ads on Instagram. With this evaluation technique we want to be able to hone in on specific issues that exist with the user experience and usability of the ads for the users. Below is the interview guide we have created for this evaluation technique. We split up our questions based on the Ad channel (Story and Newsfeed) and Ad Content to gage the overall user experience.

Heuristic Evaluation

And finally, for more insight on the third question “How do Instagram users respond to the different advertisements that appear on their Instagram newsfeed and on their Instagram stories? Do users tend to engage or respond more favorably to certain types of ad delivery (i.e. videos, photos, surveys) over others?” , as well as exploring the user experience further in terms of usability and identifying the flaws in design of Instagram ads, we have decided to conduct a Heuristic evaluation. The reason we have chosen to conduct a heuristic evaluation to explore the usability portion of the ads is because heuristic evaluations can help spot gaps and issues with relaying the information and catching the user’s eye as it comes to ads on the Instagram platform. From our user studies, we have discovered that many of the users gloss over ads (especially when they are on their stories) and when they do notice them, they are more of a nuisance than an addition to their experience. Through a thorough heuristic evaluation on the design and usability of the ads we aim to pinpoint the reasons for this. We conducted two heuristic evaluations, one using Nielsen’s and another using Amershi’s principles. Although Nielsen’s technique is very helpful and encompasses a lot of the important principles of the design of the ad experience, we also incorporate Amershi’s since it allows to explore the adaptation and learning aspects of the system.

Key Findings

Interviews

For our interviews, we each conducted 2–4 interviews using our planned interview guide. We recruited people by asking a mix of friends and acquaintances in our classes. The purpose of conducting user interviews was to understand how people feel and react to Instagram ads, and learn more about users’ knowledge of how Instagram displays ads to them. Using our guide, we had each member brief the participant on the topic before delving into the questions. The last question asks the participant to show us how they would scroll through Instagram and respond to the ads. This question allowed us to observe the participant in their everyday behavior and note their immediate reactions to the ads.

From our user interviews, we got a mix of reactions to Instagram ads. Most users felt that ads were somewhat annoying and intrusive, while a select few thought that their ads were very relevant and actually helpful in finding new products. For the former group, users said that there were too many irrelevant ads on their feed, mostly on the stories. These users felt that ads detracted from their scrolling experience on Instagram and the sheer amount of ads made them more annoying. Users rarely or never clicked on the ads, and felt that marking them as irrelevant would not do anything because they would continue to see ads anyway. One user noted that she did not expect free social media platforms to have so many ads.

However, for the latter group, users felt that ads were mostly relevant to their shopping history, and one user mentioned that he purchased his favorite pair of shoes from an Instagram ad. This user had been looking for a similar pair of shoes for quite a long period of time because stumbling upon this Instagram ad. This ad specifically noted a discount on the shoes, which promoted him to buy them immediately. Out of all 16 interviews we conducted, there were only 3 people who seriously considered purchasing an item from an ad. Other than the user who purchased directly from Instagram, the other users considered saving the link to purchase later.

When we observed users scrolling through their own Instagrams, most users did not even pause to look at the ads when they saw the sponsored word. A few users stopped to see what the product was, and if they found it interesting, noted that they thought it was interesting, but did not interact with the ad at all. One user clicked into an ad for a pair of shoes that caught her eye, but after scrolling through the site for a few seconds, realized that she did not really need the product. Overall, users did not know whether they could manage their ad preferences and just left them alone. Users noted that setting ad preferences probably would not change the experience too much because of the sheer number of ads.

In relation to class concepts, the class discussion about Netflix’s personalized home page is very similar to Instagram ads. Each user’s ad experience is unique and dependent on the data that Instagram collects about them via search history and online shopping. Similar to how Netflix shows each user different cover art, Instagram chooses different images and products that they think will get the user’s attention.

Heuristic Evaluations

For the heuristic evaluations, we conducted two evaluations — one with the Nielsen Heuristics and one with the guidelines from the Amershi et al. MSR paper. Using the tips that we discussed in class about how to conduct good evaluations, we split up the work between two team members. Those two members each used their own Instagram accounts to conduct the evaluations and see how Instagram employs different UI design techniques to make ads as effective as possible.

Using Amershi’s principles, we found that Instagram did a good job of making it clear that an ad was an ad without disrupting the visual experience of the feed. The posts and stories were Another insight that was found through this evaluation is when scrolling fast, ads are mistaken for accounts that are followed because the content is similar to something that one would normally see on the feed. This makes for a very integrated experience for the user. In regards to the difference that was noticed between Story Ads and Newsfeed Ad is that Story Ads seem to be less catered to the user and more generic while Newsfeed ads seem to be catered more to the user. In terms of the ability to interact and provide feedback on the ad experience, we found that Instagram allows the option to manage ads but does not make it outwardly available and requires the user to go through a few steps. This may be done for the purpose of making it harder to remove ads or simply get feedback on when a user doesn’t do one of the more common actions (simply scroll past it or actually interact with the ad).

From the Nielsen Heuristic Evaluation, the major insights that were gathered are as follows: first, Instagram Ads do a great job of pushing Ads to the user in a very seamless way despite it being on both their Newsfeed and their Stories. The Ads blend in without disrupting the user experience. One thought that came from this insight is that this might actually make it less likely for users to notice the ads since they fit in so well into the flow of the general user experience. This was also corroborated through our user interviews where some of the users could not recall which ads they were seeing. One feature that Instagram adds into this experience is the ability to “Hide Ad”. However, it takes a little more digging than desired to actually discover how you can manage ads on Instagram.

Literature Review

We wanted to understand how the Instagram algorithm curates its advertisements to target specific users based on their interests. To address our first two research questions, we combed through articles found online published by Instagram/Facebook that detailed the processes of curating advertisements for its users. Through online research and Instagram’s released notes on its algorithms, we gathered a couple of key takeaways that addressed our original research questions:

Are different advertisement categories (i.e. Sports, Fashion, Food, etc.) prioritized amongst different Instagram users? If so, what behavior and contextual clues does Instagram employ to identify the values in their users?

Yes, different advertisement categories are prioritized amongst different Instagram users, as Instagram is motivated to show its users advertisements from businesses that are interesting and especially relevant to each user.

As the demographic of Instagram users varies, the types of content that is shown to its users should theoretically vary as well, to be as effective and cost-efficient as possible with regards to advertisers and users of Instagram. There are different ways in which Instagram filters its content to be more relevant to its users.

What types of data does Instagram/Facebook collect from its users to curate the advertisements for individuals?

Instagram uses information about what the user does on Instagram (i.e. based on other accounts the user follows, and things the user “likes”), Facebook (likes and interests listed on Facebook), third-party sites, and other apps the user uses. To be more specific, every time a user likes or shares a post they see on Instagram, the algorithm saves this information and uses AI to categorize the content in a specific category.

To fully understand the use of Instagram advertisements by businesses, we did research on the appeal of using Instagram stories vs. news feed to display ad content to users.

What’s the appeal of using Instagram Stories to display advertisements and how do people perceive them?

According to the Internal Instagram Data published in 2019 there were around 500 million Instagrammers who are using Instagram Stories, and ⅓ of the most viewed stories come from businesses (https://business.instagram.com/a/stories-ads). Story ads provide a unique opportunity to target the ads based on the following objectives:

  • Brand awareness
  • Reach
  • Video views
  • Traffic
  • Conversions
  • App installs
  • Lead generation
  • Messages
  • Catalog sales
  • Store traffic

Another interesting feature is the “Call-to-action” options available to add to the Story Ads such as: Apply Now, Book Now, Contact Us, Download, Learn More, Get Showtimes, Request Time, See Menu, Shop Now, Sign Up, Watch More, Listen Now, and Subscribe. Instagram Story ads help drive business results through the following: 1. Creative and immersive full screen format 2. Inspires brand and direct response outcomes 3. Uses Facebook’s targeting, audience reach, and measurement solutions.

Another appeal of using Instagram Story Ads is the ability to track performance through the Insights Section of the application as well as view other metrics such as reach, impressions, and video metrics.

Recommendation

Interactive Instagram Advertisements

From our user interviews and observing how users interact with the Instagram interface, it is apparent that most users do not pay much attention to advertisements — Although the advertisements are usually at least tangentially relevant to the users’ lives, they are not usually attractive enough to draw more than just a passing glance when the user is scrolling.

In class, we talked about human computation, or adding human input to automated systems to make them more efficient. Instagram ads would be a great place to allow for more human input. Currently, it seems as if Instagram decides everything- what you see, when you see it, etc. This concept is frustrating for some users who feel that the ads they see are not relevant to what they are interested in. If we were able to allow for more user interaction, the ad experience would become more favorable for all users.

In light of these observations, we recommend that Instagram advertisements incorporate more interactive elements. This would offer a differentiating element from the regular photos and videos that users are familiar with seeing on the platform, and hence capture more attention. For example, certain aspects can be gamified such that the user is drawn to interact with the post for at least a few seconds, and absorb some of the key information while doing so.

Alternatively, the advertisement can also be formatted in the form of an interactive survey. For example, a company trying to promote beauty products can capture the audience’s attention by first asking “What kind of look are you going for today?” with the options “Natural”, “Professional” or “Glam”, and depending on the option the user clicks on, different questions will appear which will lead to different recommendations on products to purchase. This not only promotes interaction with the advertisement, but also makes the users feel like the recommendations are more personal and targeted.

Incentivizing Interactions with Advertisements

An additional feature that can promote greater interactions with Instagram advertisements is to provide small gifts and rewards for people who make purchases through clicking on Instagram advertisements. Just like how certain companies have giveaways to increase brand recognition, there can be an in-built algorithm that helps the companies recognize when the audience traffic comes from Instagram, and incentivize that through small giveaways. When users recognize that interaction with Instagram advertisements will get them small rewards, they are much more likely to interact with the posts, and higher interactions will lead to greater traction for the products promoted.

Reflection

Literature Review

A literature review of relevant materials online is always an effective method to gain more understanding on a certain topic, as we can rely on the experiments and studies that people have conducted in the past and learn from their experiences and findings. For the analysis of Instagram algorithms, which we do not have feasible access to, it is especially useful for us to peruse the information from others who might have had greater insight into the algorithms we use to analyze.

User Interviews

User interviews were particularly useful in helping us gain a greater understanding of how they interact with Instagram advertisements, the kind of products that they interact with more, and what their typical behaviors on Instagram are like. However, an area of improvement that we should have taken into consideration is that we interviewed people of mostly the same demographic — Undergraduate students who study at Cornell. People of different demographics may have different behaviors on Instagram, and different types of advertisements shown to them.

Heuristic Evaluations

The heuristic evaluations allowed us to leverage our own Instagram accounts to analyze how we felt about ads. Since every experience is different, it was valuable to examine our first-hand reactions to the ads and pay closer attention to details that we may have glossed over before. By following both Amershi and Nielsen’s design heuristics, we were able to conduct a comprehensive review of the ad system and all the functionalities that come with it. We often say that our personal experiences may be biased in these types of projects, but in this case, they provided valuable insights that helped support our final conclusions.

References

https://help.instagram.com/173081309564229

https://www.facebook.com/ads/about

https://www.digitaltrends.com/social-media/instagram-ads-interests/

https://www.facebook.com/business/help/1501370256661133?id=233103584378246

https://embedsocial.com/blog/instagram-story-ads/

https://www.facebook.com/business/help/188477725155524?id=2331035843782460

For example, certain aspects can be gamified such that the user is drawn to interact with the post for at least a few seconds, and absorb some of the key information while doing so.

Alternatively, the advertisement can also be formatted in the form of an interactive survey. For example, a company trying to promote beauty products can capture the audience’s attention by first asking “What kind of look are you going for today?” with the options “Natural”, “Professional” or “Glam”, and depending on the option the user clicks on, different questions will appear which will lead to different recommendations on products to purchase. This not only promotes interaction with the advertisement, but also makes the users feel like the recommendations are more personal and targeted.

Incentivizing Interactions with Advertisements

An additional feature that can promote greater interactions with Instagram advertisements is to provide small gifts and rewards for people who make purchases through clicking on Instagram advertisements. Just like how certain companies have giveaways to increase brand recognition, there can be an in-built algorithm that helps the companies recognize when the audience traffic comes from Instagram, and incentivize that through small giveaways. When users recognize that interaction with Instagram advertisements will get them small rewards, they are much more likely to interact with the posts, and higher interactions will lead to greater traction for the products promoted.

Reflection

Literature Review

A literature review of relevant materials online is always an effective method to gain more understanding on a certain topic, as we can rely on the experiments and studies that people have conducted in the past and learn from their experiences and findings. For the analysis of Instagram algorithms, which we do not have feasible access to, it is especially useful for us to peruse the information from others who might have had greater insight into the algorithms we use to analyze.

User Interviews

User interviews were particularly useful in helping us gain a greater understanding of how they interact with Instagram advertisements, the kind of products that they interact with more, and what their typical behaviors on Instagram are like. However, an area of improvement that we should have taken into consideration is that we interviewed people of mostly the same demographic — Undergraduate students who study at Cornell. People of different demographics may have different behaviors on Instagram, and different types of advertisements shown to them.

Heuristic Evaluations

The heuristic evaluations allowed us to leverage our own Instagram accounts to analyze how we felt about ads. Since every experience is different, it was valuable to examine our first-hand reactions to the ads and pay closer attention to details that we may have glossed over before. By following both Amershi and Nielsen’s design heuristics, we were able to conduct a comprehensive review of the ad system and all the functionalities that come with it. We often say that our personal experiences may be biased in these types of projects, but in this case, they provided valuable insights that helped support our final conclusions.

References

https://help.instagram.com/173081309564229

https://www.facebook.com/ads/about

https://www.digitaltrends.com/social-media/instagram-ads-interests/

https://www.facebook.com/business/help/1501370256661133?id=233103584378246

https://embedsocial.com/blog/instagram-story-ads/

https://www.facebook.com/business/help/188477725155524?id=2331035843782460

items.title: Venmo — A Redesign Supporting User Reminiscence
items.author: Samantha Chu
items.description:
Venmo — A Redesign Supporting User Reminiscence

How can we redesign the Venmo application to elevate connections between users?

Anna Shats, Sophia Wang, and Samantha Chu are first-year M.P.S. students studying Information Science at Cornell University.

Phase One: Identifying Project Context

Problem Statement

We want to explore the role of social connection in the realm of mobile payment platforms. How can we redesign the Venmo Application to elevate in its mission to connect its users?

Background

In recent years, the Venmo platform has become a popular payment-exchanging platform. The combination of being easy-to-use, convenient, and fun allowed the platform to gain speed amongst many millennials and generation Z users looking to exchange money between friends and businesses.

This project aims to explore the role of social connection in the realm of mobile payment platforms. The reason why we’ve chosen to focus on Venmo is due to its mission and commitment to forming these connections through an interesting venue: payment transactions. Our key contributions through this redesign is to introduce reminiscence tools into Venmo’s user interaction.

This redesign will positively impact users by facilitating and promoting continued connection.

Goals

We want to discover how to encourage reminiscence in Venmo users by incorporating a smart component to present suggestions or opportunities based on the person and or context.

We are focused on incorporating reminiscence as an added feature because through our research, we observe its prominence and success in other popular social media platforms like Facebook, Snapchat, and Instagram.

Thus, we are interested in designing a feature in the Venmo platform that can elevate the social media aspect of the payments technology platform. Our current proposed design solution path aims to support reminiscing through utilizing literature review and user insights to create an algorithm that invokes reaction from Venmo users. We have incorporated the design fiction method to critique and analyze the future of our proposed design.

Phase Two: User Research

User Interviews

To learn more about what Venmo users associate with payments and how these payments differ in different contexts, we conducted 6 user interviews, each providing valuable insight into understanding our target users.

In each user interview, we asked questions about their feelings toward reminiscence elicitors from other social media platforms they currently use, as well as how they feel these platforms play into remembering past event.

In 99% of our user interviews, users say they prefer to use text over emojis in payment descriptions.

What We Learned

  1. Other social media platforms support reminiscence, but payments allow users to remember specific occasions that may not have been posted on larger social media platforms.
  2. Photos invoke the strongest feelings of reminiscence, as they provide the user with a visual representation of a past memory.
  3. Without a clear textual description or an explanatory graphic (i.e. emojis), it’s difficult to remember what a past payment was for.

Persona

Literature Review

Based on our initial research in the Reminiscence area, we know that a few key features that need to be included in the design are: short and spontaneous triggers, inclusion of photos as memories, ability to reflect. Some others include the timing of the cues, as well as what kind of cues create the most triggering memories such as indoor/outdoor, number of people, identity of people, location, and mood.

Design Fiction

To better understand the effect of our redesign idea, we created two design fiction scenarios cards to help better understand and picture the future effect of our redesign and added features.

Positive Outcome

  • If shown during a good time for a positive memory, positive nostalgic feelings are elicited in the user.
  • Having an easy way to reach the person you requested/paid from the memories notification allows for a fast way to make that connection.
  • Reminding a user not just how much they spent, but rather what the activity was and who they did it with reminds the user that they can still enjoy social interaction despite monetary constraints.
  • An easy way to be able to look back on more memories from a specific time can help in creating the desired action in the user (to reach out and continue the social interaction offline).

Negative Outcome

  • If shown at a bad time, invoking unwanted feelings in the user can be a downside to incorporating the memories features into Venmo.
  • There should be a feature that allows the user to quickly hide an unwanted memory.
  • A negative memory may create unwanted feelings about payment transactions that are associated with a negative time in a person’s life and a loss of money.
  • If a negative memory, the social aspect of Venmo may repulse a user from using Venmo, which is contrary to what we’re trying to achieve.

Conclusions of Phase II

To bring together our findings from the user research, literature review, and design fiction we have come up with a list of key requirements, consideration, constraints, and risks we must take into account before beginning our redesign process.

Requirements:

  • The first requirement is based on our user research as well as stakeholder research and that is to improve the connective social aspect through a redesign of the current “social” features of Venmo.
  • The second key requirement is to utilize reminiscence as a tool to invoke continuous interactions between people on the social payment platform.
  • The third key requirements is the integration of the users’ previous history of transactions as well as other sources of data that may be helpful.

Considerations:

  • How can we facilitate more of a sense of connection in a realm that can be sensitive (economic insecurity)?
  • How do we encourage using Venmo for its social aspect as opposed to other competitors?
  • How do we maintain Venmo’s sleek design and not overcrowd the current interface with a new feature?
  • How do we use reminiscence to invoke a stronger connection with the user base?
  • How do we/ should we administer different levels of reminiscence (just based on user history vs. presenting a photo at a time the transactions were made, etc.)
  • Private vs public vs semi-public data on Venmo is a user’s choice. WOuld this affect our reminiscence feature?

Constraints:

  • Hard to conduct user interviews as well as usability testing since they cannot be conducted in person.
  • Ability to access people’s information outside of the Venmo platform if they do not consent, in which case, this won’t really work.
  • Time constraints

Risks:

  • Security is always a very big risk with mobile payments. Leakage of confidential user information (especially since now we would be asking for even more permissions than what Venmo currently utilizes).
  • People may not want to consent to such a feature.

Phase Three: Redesign

Our current proposed design solution path aims to influence user behavior through the tool of reminiscing. This changes the way users will perceive the platform, from being just a payments platform to a platform that encourages users to connect with one another as well as supports reminiscing on past memories.

Our design towards a Venmo platform that supports reminiscence fulfills the requirements that we discovered in our user insights. From our user interviews, we learned that users recall reminiscence tools to be a positive feature in other platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and Snapchat. We incorporated similar elements in our Venmo redesign.

Low-Fidelity Prototype

High-Fidelity Prototype

Proposed Value of this Redesign

We want to study and improve the process of sending and receiving payments as well as ways to look back on previous payments. The current design allows for a social experience, but only if the user actively engages with the app by leaving detailed messages and comments. We want to look at ways that a new design could encourage and incentivize users to become more active in the social experience of Venmo.

It is interesting to look into how Venmo can be improved as a social media platform because after doing some research, our team notes that many payment processing/transferring applications now do not explore the social aspect of their design, and Venmo is the pioneer for intersecting the two: social media and payments. Because of this, Venmo has reached prevalence fairly quickly, especially amongst the younger population (Gen Z and Millennials). In addition, many stores and businesses that cater to these generations have added Venmo as a payment method because of its growing popularity as well as its user-friendly design and efficiency in payment. Since Venmo’s primary mission is to elevate connections and empower its users the proposed value by this new redesign is the elevation of the social aspect of Venmo to change the course of the payments technology landscape.

We hope that by studying an example of one unconventional social media platform, we can start a conversation about how other apps and services might contribute to the digital social experience as well.

Phase Four: Evaluation

Heuristic Evaluations

We will be using Nielsen’s Heuristics to test our prototype and make sure the user experience is seamless and usable. By conducting HEs, we hope to identify any weaknesses in our design that users’ may struggle with. We can also justify that certain design decisions will work well for the user. By seeing which heuristics were not met, we can iterate on our design.

Evaluation Insights

From the heuristic evaluation, we identified the parts of our design that failed the test (above), mainly associated with undo and error prevention. Going back to our design, we decided to add options for the user to exit out of the enlarged picture as well as delete a shared memory.

Phase Five: Iteration and Reflection

Class Concepts

  • One concept that we utilized in our design is after reading Professor Dan Cosley’s paper in class about context-aware reminiscing. This helped us shape what kind of information we might want to add, interactions it would prompt, and benefits from reminiscing.
  • After reading Google’s People+AU Guidebook in class, specifically after the chapter on user needs + Defining Success, another concept that we introduced into our process specifically during our user interviews is asking our users to go through the workflow of their normal interactions. What this helped us figure is where and in what manner our feature could naturally flow, as well as deciding whether AI’s ability to personalize for each user is useful for our context.
  • Nielsen’s Heuristic Evaluation is yet another concept that we learned in class and incorporated into our design process to evaluate our design.

Work Context

The redesign and user research efforts were done in a class centered around designing intelligent user interfaces at Cornell University. Due to the global pandemic that surfaced during the middle of the project timeline, our research was stymied by a couple of constraints such as our inability to conduct in-person group meetings, user interviews, and usability testing. Our entire cycle of research was done virtually through zoom meetings. As there were no ways to conduct an in-person usability testing session with potential users, we decided to forego this method of evaluation as we felt a virtual session would not produce the same kind of valuable insight. Therefore, we decided to utilize the heuristic evaluation method to reflect on our redesign and possible improvements for our next iteration. In the future, if circumstances permit, it would be useful to conduct in-person usability testing sessions with target users and observe how they interact with the redesigned platform.

It was challenging to adapt to the circumstances but we believe that our redesign would be a valuable feature addition to the existing Venmo application.

References

Finley, J. R., Naaz, F., & Goh, F. W. (2018). Memory and technology. Cham: Springer International Publishing. https://doi. org/10.1007/978–3–319–99169–6.

Peesapati, S. T., Schwanda, V., Schultz, J., Lepage, M., Jeong, S. Y., & Cosley, D. (2010, April). Pensieve: supporting everyday reminiscence. In Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (pp. 2027–2036).

Van Gennip, D., Van Den Hoven, E., & Markopoulos, P. (2015, April). Things that make us reminisce: Everyday memory cues as opportunities for interaction design. In Proceedings of the 33rd Annual ACM Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (pp. 3443–3452).

items.title: Lychee: Connecting International Chinese Students with Americans through Language.
items.author: Samantha Chu
items.description:
1. Addressing Our Design Problem

Attending a new school can be intimidating, especially if you’re a young adult seeking education opportunities in a country halfway around the world, a place where the spoken language is not native to you. These are real-life problems that many students who attend universities face, and is an underrecognized need, as many institutions do not address the need for these students to integrate themselves into the “americanized” parts of campus. Our project targets International students from China who attend college or university in the United States, who hold the problem of having little to no experience with speaking conversational English with American students their age.

The design problem we are addressing is the lack of resources for International Chinese students to learn conversational English. These conversational skills are separate from the typical language skills that they might learn in a standard ESL (English as a second Language) course. Especially on social media, college students colloquially use many phrases and terms that exist and gain speed in popularity online; This means that people who aren’t a part of these established online communities would not understand, nor be able to relate to others in that sense. We believe that the lack of these conversational skills prohibits this target audience from pursuing deeper relationships with a wider range of American students. Our app addresses this problem with this proposed solution: Make the process of learning conversational English accessible, fun, and easy. This solution contributes to a larger purpose of encouraging diverse relationships on campus, as well as overcoming and breaking free of deep-rooted stereotypes that are perpetuated towards the International student community.

Our goal for this project is to help better integrate students from China into university campuses by providing a means in which they can learn conversational English terms that American students use on a day-to-day basis. To solve this problem, we designed the app, Lychee. In Lychee, these students can take English lessons, review material through quizzes, use a dictionary, as well as practice their English skills by chatting with an AI bot. The goal of our app is to give International Chinese students the confidence and motivation to expand their network, as well as feel more comfortable in the activities they choose to pursue. Our team had several motivating factors for solving this design problem. Though our project group is diverse in many aspects, we all share a similar Asian heritage. As an all Asian-American team, we serve as the medium; we recognize the need for International Chinese students to be better exposed to the English language so that they feel they have the abilities to expand their network of friends and colleagues, as well as be successful in their endeavors on campus.

2. Human-Centered Design Process

A. Understanding Users: Learning about the Audience

Understanding the experiences of users of our design is critical in creating a good product. Through interviews, we attempted to understand the needs of International Chinese students within the context of the design challenge to “weave the threads of the social fabric.” We interpreted this to mean connecting the experiences of many to form a more inclusive, diverse and complete community. Creating a fully weaved “blanket” (in reference to the fabric metaphor), if you will. We found that International Chinese students possess the need to understand American culture in a way that is separate from what they’ve learned about Americans in their high schools in China.

From our preliminary interviews, we found that when coming to the United States, many International Chinese students look to other students from China to form friendships because of their common backgrounds. Similarly, one interviewee mentioned how it is simply easier for her to understand what is being said when she with other International Chinese friends. The target audience values creating friendships with people that understand how and what they are communicating. Additionally, another interviewee, who came to the United States at the start of college, mentioned that there aren’t many resources for her to understand American culture in a non-academic context. In this stage, we were focused on understanding the needs and wants of our users.

B. Design: Exploring the Solution Space

As part of our design journey in coming up with an effective solution for our design problem, we created an affinity diagram to analyze the root problem, as drawn from our contextual interviews. We separated our field notes into several categories: Background, Clubs, Academics, Friends, Experiences, Languages, Cornell Resources, and Suggestions. These categories gave us a better idea of how international Chinese students feel about different parts of their college experiences.

Figure 1: Affinity Diagram

Separating our main interview take-away points in this order helped us explore the solution space and come up with several solutions to the problem using the “Five Why’s” method.

Problem: International Students have a hard time building a social and professional network at Cornell.

  1. Why? They feel distant from other groups on campus.
  2. Why? They don’t feel as comfortable using English when public speaking, talking in meetings or interviews.
  3. Why? Chinese is their mother tongue and they use Chinese more often in their daily lives.
  4. Why? They haven’t had as many opportunities to use English in a more casual setting.
  5. Why? They have not learned English as a conversational language as much in an Academic setting.

The core problem we identified was that they have not had the opportunities to learn about American vernacular in conversational settings that are relevant to the age group of College students, and therefore are less comfortable interacting with people that aren’t also International Chinese. We individually came up with 20 design idea sketches, and presented them to each other in order to come up with our final design. The solution we settled on was to design an app that teaches users conversational English through lessons and quizzes. In addition, the app would provide a dictionary for users to look up common English words and also give users the opportunity to practice and apply what they’ve learned through an AI messaging feature. We felt that this solution was different from what we have found in the current solution space, as this solution is more technological and more relevant to daily life.

To develop our design concept, we decided on the important tasks our users should be able to complete in the app (login/make account, view lesson, look up words in the dictionary, chat with American student AI, change account settings) and how they’d relate to each other, mapping out the flow between each task. We then composed a scenario, sketched storyboards to visualize the story and each task, and created UI sketches of each screen. Through this, we were able to start thinking about the look of the app and how we wanted to design it, and think about the user’s interaction with the app.

C. Implement: Prototyping and Building our Design & Evaluate: Reflecting on Design and Receiving Feedback from Users

We created three different types of prototypes (paper, Balsamiq, and InVision) of our design. With each prototype, we conducted evaluations in order to understand what the users think about the design, see how they interact with it, and find problems in the design. Using the insights gained from the evaluations, we were able to go back and improve our designs for the next iteration.

Low-Fidelity Paper Prototype

Figure 2: Paper prototype — lesson page

Using post-it notes and construction paper, our team constructed a low-fidelity representation of the features we were trying to implement. At this point in the human-centered design process, we created a quick version of our final product so that we could iterate in our design and do a quick user-test in order to work out the kinks and start the Balsamiq prototype. We created each screen using post-it notes, and had three participants interact with the features by manually switching the post-it note screens when the user clicked on any icon. We gave them six tasks: set up an account, log in, start a conversation with the American student AI, search for a word, and change the settings. These were the major functions that we included in our app, and having these participants do a quick run through of the paper prototype helped to point out any big problems we’d need to address. From the user feedback, we learned that the overall structure and flow was good, but there were some inconsistencies with the design across all pages. In addition, the users gave some good suggestions that we decided to implement in the next prototype, including unlocking all lesson levels so that users of different skill levels can use it and adding additional functions like logout and changing system language.

Mid-Fidelity Prototype with Balsamiq Platform

Figure 3: Balsamiq Prototype — lesson page

Using Balsamiq, our team constructed a medium-fidelity representation of our design ideas. Making this prototype allowed us to create an effective representation of the features we wanted to include in our final product. We were able to create a more interactive, clean, and easier to use prototype. With our Balsamiq prototype, we conducted heuristic evaluations to identify usability problems in the design. We individually went through the prototype, looked for design elements that violated Nielsen’s heuristics, and recorded them. Then we merged all of our problems as a group, and went through and identified the major ones to report and figured out which ones we wanted to address. Some of the key insights that we gained from this step was to make UI elements consistent, navigation bar icons more distinguishable, make error messages more specific, make pop up windows more informative, and implement UI elements such as a progress bar that would give users more feedback.

We also conducted a design walkthrough in class, where we presented our paper prototype (as we were in the progress of making the Balsamiq prototype) to our fellow designer peers and gathered any feedback from them. One of the suggestions that we got that we decided to implement in InVision prototype was to add suggested/trending words for the dictionary search function, which we felt was a good idea because it encouraged users to learn even more. Another was to change the positions of the navigation icons to have a natural flow, such as having the first page be on the left side of the navigation bar instead of in the middle. That way, the relative position can help indicate the flow and not confuse the user. Though we got many good suggestions, we decided that these were the ones we felt that we needed to include in our design.

High-Fidelity Prototype with Sketch and Invision Platform

Figure 4: InVision Prototype — lesson page

We implemented the design changes from the Balsamiq prototype heuristic evaluations and design walkthrough in our first InVision prototype. We used Sketch to make the different screens and then linked them on InVision. Through this prototype, we were able to establish a definitive brand identity for the app (app name, color palette, font families, margins, logo, and overall aesthetic). Then, we conducted usability testing with this prototype. We had four study goals for this evaluation: test the user satisfaction with the interaction with the app; test the effectiveness of the app in helping the user learn conversational English and American culture; test the ease of use of the app; find design problems that need to be fixed. We wanted to use the metrics satisfaction, effectiveness, engagement, learnability, usability, and error rate to look at the interaction between the user and app. We recruited four participants for this step from the target user group, and asked them several pre-test questions about demographics, gave them several tasks outlined below, and asked them post-test open-ended questions to gain some more feedback.

Tasks:

  • Create an account and log into the app.
  • Now that you’re logged in, you want to learn a lesson. Start and complete lesson 2.
  • Say you find the word “finna” confusing, look it up in the dictionary and favorite it.
  • Now that you have learned new material, chat with the AI bot Nick by responding to his message with “wuts up bro I’m Yu Xuan from Shanghai, China”. Once you are done, remove the conversation.
  • Now you want to change your password to lychee123.

From this user testing, we gained valuable insights about the interaction and made changes accordingly. We learned that although the overall flow, design, and interaction was good, there were some slight changes that we could make to better improve the app. First, we realized that users still had some trouble with the navigation icons, however, they were able to easily recover from their mistakes and was able to figure out the navigation bar after the first try. To solve this, we decided to implement a short tutorial after the user logs in for the first time, so that the user can know the different symbols and functions before they start interacting with the app. Another problem was that users had trouble identifying and also pressing buttons on the prototype. For example, all the participants had trouble figuring out where the delete conversation button was located. Thus, we decided to make the buttons more clear by making them larger and also more indicative (such as adding an “X” on the delete conversation button). The last change was in regards to the chat page. Many participants had mixed feelings about this function, as an AI was cool and interesting, but also kind of awkward. So, we decided to change the design and give the users an option to chat with an AI or other users of the app on this page. This gives them some more freedom but also maintains the original intent of letting the users practice what they learned.

Through all the different evaluations that we conducted, we were able to gain a lot of valuable insights and make important, beneficial changes to our prototypes. We realized that as designers, we think that we have covered all the bases, but these sessions are crucial as we can get a fresh viewpoint and also better understand our users.
items.content:
1. Addressing Our Design Problem

Attending a new school can be intimidating, especially if you’re a young adult seeking education opportunities in a country halfway around the world, a place where the spoken language is not native to you. These are real-life problems that many students who attend universities face, and is an underrecognized need, as many institutions do not address the need for these students to integrate themselves into the “americanized” parts of campus. Our project targets International students from China who attend college or university in the United States, who hold the problem of having little to no experience with speaking conversational English with American students their age.

The design problem we are addressing is the lack of resources for International Chinese students to learn conversational English. These conversational skills are separate from the typical language skills that they might learn in a standard ESL (English as a second Language) course. Especially on social media, college students colloquially use many phrases and terms that exist and gain speed in popularity online; This means that people who aren’t a part of these established online communities would not understand, nor be able to relate to others in that sense. We believe that the lack of these conversational skills prohibits this target audience from pursuing deeper relationships with a wider range of American students. Our app addresses this problem with this proposed solution: Make the process of learning conversational English accessible, fun, and easy. This solution contributes to a larger purpose of encouraging diverse relationships on campus, as well as overcoming and breaking free of deep-rooted stereotypes that are perpetuated towards the International student community.

Our goal for this project is to help better integrate students from China into university campuses by providing a means in which they can learn conversational English terms that American students use on a day-to-day basis. To solve this problem, we designed the app, Lychee. In Lychee, these students can take English lessons, review material through quizzes, use a dictionary, as well as practice their English skills by chatting with an AI bot. The goal of our app is to give International Chinese students the confidence and motivation to expand their network, as well as feel more comfortable in the activities they choose to pursue. Our team had several motivating factors for solving this design problem. Though our project group is diverse in many aspects, we all share a similar Asian heritage. As an all Asian-American team, we serve as the medium; we recognize the need for International Chinese students to be better exposed to the English language so that they feel they have the abilities to expand their network of friends and colleagues, as well as be successful in their endeavors on campus.

2. Human-Centered Design Process

A. Understanding Users: Learning about the Audience

Understanding the experiences of users of our design is critical in creating a good product. Through interviews, we attempted to understand the needs of International Chinese students within the context of the design challenge to “weave the threads of the social fabric.” We interpreted this to mean connecting the experiences of many to form a more inclusive, diverse and complete community. Creating a fully weaved “blanket” (in reference to the fabric metaphor), if you will. We found that International Chinese students possess the need to understand American culture in a way that is separate from what they’ve learned about Americans in their high schools in China.

From our preliminary interviews, we found that when coming to the United States, many International Chinese students look to other students from China to form friendships because of their common backgrounds. Similarly, one interviewee mentioned how it is simply easier for her to understand what is being said when she with other International Chinese friends. The target audience values creating friendships with people that understand how and what they are communicating. Additionally, another interviewee, who came to the United States at the start of college, mentioned that there aren’t many resources for her to understand American culture in a non-academic context. In this stage, we were focused on understanding the needs and wants of our users.

B. Design: Exploring the Solution Space

As part of our design journey in coming up with an effective solution for our design problem, we created an affinity diagram to analyze the root problem, as drawn from our contextual interviews. We separated our field notes into several categories: Background, Clubs, Academics, Friends, Experiences, Languages, Cornell Resources, and Suggestions. These categories gave us a better idea of how international Chinese students feel about different parts of their college experiences.

Figure 1: Affinity Diagram

Separating our main interview take-away points in this order helped us explore the solution space and come up with several solutions to the problem using the “Five Why’s” method.

Problem: International Students have a hard time building a social and professional network at Cornell.

  1. Why? They feel distant from other groups on campus.
  2. Why? They don’t feel as comfortable using English when public speaking, talking in meetings or interviews.
  3. Why? Chinese is their mother tongue and they use Chinese more often in their daily lives.
  4. Why? They haven’t had as many opportunities to use English in a more casual setting.
  5. Why? They have not learned English as a conversational language as much in an Academic setting.

The core problem we identified was that they have not had the opportunities to learn about American vernacular in conversational settings that are relevant to the age group of College students, and therefore are less comfortable interacting with people that aren’t also International Chinese. We individually came up with 20 design idea sketches, and presented them to each other in order to come up with our final design. The solution we settled on was to design an app that teaches users conversational English through lessons and quizzes. In addition, the app would provide a dictionary for users to look up common English words and also give users the opportunity to practice and apply what they’ve learned through an AI messaging feature. We felt that this solution was different from what we have found in the current solution space, as this solution is more technological and more relevant to daily life.

To develop our design concept, we decided on the important tasks our users should be able to complete in the app (login/make account, view lesson, look up words in the dictionary, chat with American student AI, change account settings) and how they’d relate to each other, mapping out the flow between each task. We then composed a scenario, sketched storyboards to visualize the story and each task, and created UI sketches of each screen. Through this, we were able to start thinking about the look of the app and how we wanted to design it, and think about the user’s interaction with the app.

C. Implement: Prototyping and Building our Design & Evaluate: Reflecting on Design and Receiving Feedback from Users

We created three different types of prototypes (paper, Balsamiq, and InVision) of our design. With each prototype, we conducted evaluations in order to understand what the users think about the design, see how they interact with it, and find problems in the design. Using the insights gained from the evaluations, we were able to go back and improve our designs for the next iteration.

Low-Fidelity Paper Prototype

Figure 2: Paper prototype — lesson page

Using post-it notes and construction paper, our team constructed a low-fidelity representation of the features we were trying to implement. At this point in the human-centered design process, we created a quick version of our final product so that we could iterate in our design and do a quick user-test in order to work out the kinks and start the Balsamiq prototype. We created each screen using post-it notes, and had three participants interact with the features by manually switching the post-it note screens when the user clicked on any icon. We gave them six tasks: set up an account, log in, start a conversation with the American student AI, search for a word, and change the settings. These were the major functions that we included in our app, and having these participants do a quick run through of the paper prototype helped to point out any big problems we’d need to address. From the user feedback, we learned that the overall structure and flow was good, but there were some inconsistencies with the design across all pages. In addition, the users gave some good suggestions that we decided to implement in the next prototype, including unlocking all lesson levels so that users of different skill levels can use it and adding additional functions like logout and changing system language.

Mid-Fidelity Prototype with Balsamiq Platform

Figure 3: Balsamiq Prototype — lesson page

Using Balsamiq, our team constructed a medium-fidelity representation of our design ideas. Making this prototype allowed us to create an effective representation of the features we wanted to include in our final product. We were able to create a more interactive, clean, and easier to use prototype. With our Balsamiq prototype, we conducted heuristic evaluations to identify usability problems in the design. We individually went through the prototype, looked for design elements that violated Nielsen’s heuristics, and recorded them. Then we merged all of our problems as a group, and went through and identified the major ones to report and figured out which ones we wanted to address. Some of the key insights that we gained from this step was to make UI elements consistent, navigation bar icons more distinguishable, make error messages more specific, make pop up windows more informative, and implement UI elements such as a progress bar that would give users more feedback.

We also conducted a design walkthrough in class, where we presented our paper prototype (as we were in the progress of making the Balsamiq prototype) to our fellow designer peers and gathered any feedback from them. One of the suggestions that we got that we decided to implement in InVision prototype was to add suggested/trending words for the dictionary search function, which we felt was a good idea because it encouraged users to learn even more. Another was to change the positions of the navigation icons to have a natural flow, such as having the first page be on the left side of the navigation bar instead of in the middle. That way, the relative position can help indicate the flow and not confuse the user. Though we got many good suggestions, we decided that these were the ones we felt that we needed to include in our design.

High-Fidelity Prototype with Sketch and Invision Platform

Figure 4: InVision Prototype — lesson page

We implemented the design changes from the Balsamiq prototype heuristic evaluations and design walkthrough in our first InVision prototype. We used Sketch to make the different screens and then linked them on InVision. Through this prototype, we were able to establish a definitive brand identity for the app (app name, color palette, font families, margins, logo, and overall aesthetic). Then, we conducted usability testing with this prototype. We had four study goals for this evaluation: test the user satisfaction with the interaction with the app; test the effectiveness of the app in helping the user learn conversational English and American culture; test the ease of use of the app; find design problems that need to be fixed. We wanted to use the metrics satisfaction, effectiveness, engagement, learnability, usability, and error rate to look at the interaction between the user and app. We recruited four participants for this step from the target user group, and asked them several pre-test questions about demographics, gave them several tasks outlined below, and asked them post-test open-ended questions to gain some more feedback.

Tasks:

  • Create an account and log into the app.
  • Now that you’re logged in, you want to learn a lesson. Start and complete lesson 2.
  • Say you find the word “finna” confusing, look it up in the dictionary and favorite it.
  • Now that you have learned new material, chat with the AI bot Nick by responding to his message with “wuts up bro I’m Yu Xuan from Shanghai, China”. Once you are done, remove the conversation.
  • Now you want to change your password to lychee123.

From this user testing, we gained valuable insights about the interaction and made changes accordingly. We learned that although the overall flow, design, and interaction was good, there were some slight changes that we could make to better improve the app. First, we realized that users still had some trouble with the navigation icons, however, they were able to easily recover from their mistakes and was able to figure out the navigation bar after the first try. To solve this, we decided to implement a short tutorial after the user logs in for the first time, so that the user can know the different symbols and functions before they start interacting with the app. Another problem was that users had trouble identifying and also pressing buttons on the prototype. For example, all the participants had trouble figuring out where the delete conversation button was located. Thus, we decided to make the buttons more clear by making them larger and also more indicative (such as adding an “X” on the delete conversation button). The last change was in regards to the chat page. Many participants had mixed feelings about this function, as an AI was cool and interesting, but also kind of awkward. So, we decided to change the design and give the users an option to chat with an AI or other users of the app on this page. This gives them some more freedom but also maintains the original intent of letting the users practice what they learned.

Through all the different evaluations that we conducted, we were able to gain a lot of valuable insights and make important, beneficial changes to our prototypes. We realized that as designers, we think that we have covered all the bases, but these sessions are crucial as we can get a fresh viewpoint and also better understand our users.
